Koetsier, J, Cavill, R, Reijnders, R, Harvey, J, Homann, J, Kouhsar, M, Deckers, K, Köhler, S, Eijssen, L M T, van den Hove, D L A, Demuth, I, Düzel, S, Smith, R G, Smith, A R, Burrage, J, Walker, E M, Shireby, G, Hannon, E, Dempster, E, Frayling, T, Mill, J, Dobricic, V, Johannsen, P, Wittig, M, Franke, A, Vandenberghe, R, Schaeverbeke, J, Freund-Levi, Y, Frölich, L, Scheltens, P, Teunissen, C E, Frisoni, G, Blin, O, Richardson, J C, Bordet, R, Engelborghs, S, de Roeck, E, Martinez-Lage, P, Tainta, M, Lleó, A, Sala, I, Popp, J, Peyratout, G, Verhey, F, Tsolaki, M, Andreasson, U, Blennow, K, Zetterberg, H, Streffer, J, Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), Vos, S, Visser, P & Pishva, E 2024, 'Blood-based multivariate methylation risk score for cognitive impairment and dementia', Alzheimer's & Dementia, vol. 20, no. 10, pp. 6682-6698. https://doi.org/10.1002/alz.14061

crossref.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:sec><jats:title>INTRODUCTION</jats:title><jats:p>The established link between DNA methylation and pathophysiology of dementia, along with its potential role as a molecular mediator of lifestyle and environmental influences, positions blood‐derived DNA methylation as a promising tool for early dementia risk detection.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>METHODS</jats:title><jats:p>In conjunction with an extensive array of machine learning techniques, we employed whole blood genome‐wide DNA methylation data as a surrogate for 14 modifiable and non‐modifiable factors in the assessment of dementia risk in independent dementia cohorts.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title><jats:p>We established a multivariate methylation risk score (MMRS) for identifying mild cognitive impairment cross‐sectionally, independent of age and sex (<jats:italic>P</jats:italic> = 2.0 × 10<jats:sup>−3</jats:sup>). This score significantly predicted the prospective development of cognitive impairments in independent studies of Alzheimer's disease (hazard ratio for Rey's Auditory Verbal Learning Test (RAVLT)‐Learning = 2.47) and Parkinson's disease (hazard ratio for MCI/dementia<jats:sub> </jats:sub>= 2.59).</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>DISCUSSION</jats:title><jats:p>Our work shows the potential of employing blood‐derived DNA methylation data in the assessment of dementia risk.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Highlights</jats:title><jats:p><jats:list list-type="bullet">
<jats:list-item><jats:p>We used whole blood DNA methylation as a surrogate for 14 dementia risk factors.</jats:p></jats:list-item>
<jats:list-item><jats:p>Created a multivariate methylation risk score for predicting cognitive impairment.</jats:p></jats:list-item>
<jats:list-item><jats:p>Emphasized the role of machine learning and omics data in predicting dementia.</jats:p></jats:list-item>
<jats:list-item><jats:p>The score predicts cognitive impairment development at the population level.</jats:p></jats:list-item>
</jats:list></jats:p></jats:sec>
title: Blood‐based multivariate methylation risk score for cognitive impairment and dementia
